topics.title: What is SQL?
topics.description: Understand what SQL is, what a relational database is, and how to write your first SELECT statement.

lessons.title: Relational databases
lessons.body: A relational database stores data in tables — rows and columns, like a spreadsheet. SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language used to interact with these databases.

lessons.title: Your first SELECT
lessons.body: SELECT retrieves data from a table. * means 'all columns'. FROM specifies which table to read.

lessons.title: Selecting specific columns
lessons.body: List column names separated by commas to return only the data you need.

lessons.title: Filtering with WHERE
lessons.body: The WHERE clause filters rows. Only rows where the condition is TRUE are returned.
lessons.code: SELECT name FROM users WHERE age > 18;

topics.title: JOINs
topics.description: Combine rows from two or more tables using SQL JOIN operations.

lessons.title: Why JOINs?
lessons.body: Data is often spread across multiple tables to avoid duplication. JOINs let you combine them based on a matching column (usually a foreign key).

lessons.title: INNER JOIN
lessons.body: INNER JOIN returns only rows that have a match in both tables.

lessons.title: LEFT JOIN
lessons.body: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and the matching rows from the right. If there is no match, NULL is returned for the right table's columns.

lessons.title: Aliases
lessons.body: Use AS to give tables or columns shorter names, making queries easier to read.

topics.title: Aggregates & GROUP BY
topics.description: Summarise data with aggregate functions and group rows with GROUP BY.

lessons.title: Aggregate functions
lessons.body: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX compute a single value from a set of rows.
lessons.code:
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders;
SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees;

lessons.title: GROUP BY
lessons.body: GROUP BY collapses multiple rows with the same value into one group, so aggregates apply per group.

lessons.title: HAVING
lessons.body: WHERE filters rows before grouping. HAVING filters groups after aggregation.
lessons.code:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

lessons.title: NULL handling
lessons.body: Aggregate functions ignore NULL values. COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(column) skips NULLs in that column.

topics.title: Window Functions
topics.description: Compute values across a sliding window of rows without collapsing them, using SQL window functions.

lessons.title: What is a window function?
lessons.body: A window function computes a value for each row based on a set of related rows (the window), without collapsing them into a single output row. The OVER clause defines the window.

lessons.title: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK
lessons.body: Ranking functions assign a position to each row within a partition. RANK() skips numbers after ties; DENSE_RANK() does not.

lessons.title: PARTITION BY
lessons.body: PARTITION BY divides rows into groups before the window function is applied, like GROUP BY but without collapsing rows.

lessons.title: Running totals with SUM OVER
lessons.body: SUM with ORDER BY inside OVER computes a cumulative total up to the current row.
